name: McLaughlin, Anne Collins
recent_1: Pak, R., Rovira, E., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Polite AI mitigates user susceptibility to AI hallucinations. Ergonomics, 68(10), 1735–1745. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604
recent_1_synopsis: In a between-subjects experiment, participants completed a difficult quiz aided by either a polite or neutral-toned AI chatbot that occasionally provided hallucinated (incorrect) information. Signal-detection analysis showed that users interacting with the polite AI were modestly more sensitive at detecting hallucinations and adopted a more conservative response bias, suggesting conversational tone as a design lever for improving critical evaluation of generative-AI output.
recent_2: McLaughlin, A. C., Coleman, M. G., Byrne, V., Benton, R., Lodge, F., & Patten, T. (2025). Cognitive aid design using diminished reality to support selective attention by reducing distraction. Human Factors, 67(9), 937–961. DOI: 10.1177/00187208251325169
recent_2_synopsis: This study tested whether "diminished reality" interfaces—which visually suppress task-irrelevant information rather than adding cues—can support selective attention by reducing distraction during a complex task. It extends cognition-aid design principles toward augmented/diminished reality, showing how removing visual clutter can offload attentional demands.
recent_3: Matalenas, L. A., & McLaughlin, A. C. (2025). Training flexible spatial-cognitive estimation strategies using augmented reality. Ergonomics, 68(3), 425–443. DOI: 10.1080/00140139.2024.2332768
recent_3_synopsis: Two experiments examined whether augmented reality can train transferable spatial-estimation skills, using portion estimation as the test case and manipulating whether AR scaffolded a decomposition strategy versus a no-AR control. The work demonstrates how AR can teach flexible cognitive strategies that persist beyond the aided condition, informing the design of training versus crutch-style aids.
seminal_1: Green, C. S., Bavelier, D., Kramer, A. F., Vinogradov, S., Ansorge, U., Ball, K. K., Bingel, U., Chein, J. M., Colzato, L. S., Edwards, J. D., Facoetti, A., Gazzaley, A., Gathercole, S. E., Ghisletta, P., Gori, S., Granic, I., Hillman, C. H., Hommel, B., Jaeggi, S. M., … Witt, C. M. (2019). Improving methodological standards in behavioral interventions for cognitive enhancement. Journal of Cognitive Enhancement, 3(1), 2–29. DOI: 10.1007/s41465-018-0115-y
seminal_1_synopsis: This large multi-author consensus paper, on which McLaughlin is a co-author, sets out widely agreed-upon best methodological practices for behavioral cognitive-enhancement interventions—covering control-group design, feasibility versus efficacy versus effectiveness studies, outcome reporting, and dissemination. With roughly 315 citations (Google Scholar), it is her most-cited work and serves as a foundational rigor standard for the brain-training and cognitive-training literature.
seminal_2: Rogers, W. A., Fisk, A. D., McLaughlin, A. C., & Pak, R. (2005). Touch a screen or turn a knob: Choosing the best device for the job. Human Factors, 47(2), 271–288. DOI: 10.1518/0018720054679452
seminal_2_synopsis: This study assessed how task demands and user age affect performance with a direct input device (touch screen) versus an indirect device (rotary knob), finding that the optimal device depends jointly on the task and the user's age. With roughly 239 citations (Google Scholar), it is a foundational empirical reference for age-aware input-device selection and human factors of interface control design.
expertise_overview: Studies how learning and cognition change with age and how interfaces, training programs, and cognitive aids can be designed to support older and younger adults. Core topics include attention control and selective attention, working memory and individual differences in cognitive ability, the motivation for and benefits of cognitively complex activities, and training people to use technology. Applies these to game-based and digital-game training, augmented and diminished reality aids, input-device and display design, and increasingly to human interaction with AI and automation (trust, reliance, AI etiquette, and susceptibility to AI hallucinations). Methodologically she favors controlled behavioral experiments, randomized intervention designs, signal-detection and individual-differences analyses, and rigorous methodological standards for cognitive-training research. Application domains span aging and gerontechnology, health and medical devices, education, and military/defense systems.
notes: Identity confirmed with high confidence. Matched via Google Scholar profile (user=gGh0i8AAAAAJ), NC State CHASS faculty page (chass.ncsu.edu/people/acmclaug), and her LACElab site (acmclaug.wordpress.ncsu.edu); the seed hf_papers (e.g., the 2025 diminished-reality and 2023 attention-control papers) appear directly on her Scholar profile, anchoring disambiguation. She is a Human Factors / cognitive-aging researcher at NC State (LACElab), co-author with Wendy Rogers/Richard Pak of "Designing Displays for Older Adults," and an Associate Editor of Human Factors. Citation counts and recency are from Google Scholar as of June 2026 (total citations ~3,628, h-index 29). All cited DOIs verified via Crossref (HTTP 200, matching title): 10.1080/00140139.2024.2434604, 10.1177/00187208251325169 (pre-verified seed), 10.1080/00140139.2024.2332768, 10.1007/s41465-018-0115-y, 10.1518/0018720054679452. No unverifiable citations. Note that recent_2 and recent_3 are dated 2025 by journal issue but appeared online in 2024.
